Be My AI:The image shows a section titled "About the Artists" with two photographs and accompanying text.

On the left, there is a black and white photo of Wei Zhu holding a violin with a cityscape in the background. The text below reads:

Wei Zhu
Violinist Wei Zhu began to study violin at an early age. At age 13, he was accepted into the school affiliated with the Central Conservatory of Music in Beijing. In 2015, he was admitted by The Juilliard School, where he studied with Hyo Kang and Adele Anthony to pursue the Bachelor of Music degree.

Zhu entered Yale School of Music in 2019 for his Master of Music degree, followed by Master of Musical Arts Degree with Augustin Hadelich in 2021. He was the recipient of Broadus Erle prize for outstanding violinists twice. In 2022, Zhu was accepted by The Peabody Institute of John Hopkins University to continue his study for Doctor of Musical Arts degree with Vadim Gluzman.

Throughout his academic and professional career, Zhu has participated in masterclasses with renowned musicians like Pinchas Zukerman and Noah Bendix-Balgley.

On the right, there is a black and white photo of Hui-Chuan Chen smiling. The text below reads:

Hui-Chuan Chen
Pianist Hui-Chuan Chen leads a diverse career as a concert pianist, chamber musician, and educator. She has performed widely throughout the U.S. and abroad and has appeared at major performance venues including Carnegie Hall, The Kennedy Center, and the Supreme Court. She has featured at the Aspen Music Festival, National Gallery of Art, The Phillips Collection, Bargemusic Festival, The Accademia Musicale Chigiana, The Walters Arts Museum, An Die Musik, and the Amalfi Coast International Music Festival.

Chen has collaborated with distinguished artists including Amit Peled, Marina Piccinini, and Chee-Yun Kim, Jennifer Frautschi, and others. She is a long-term partner of the Washington International Piano Competition, The National Society of Arts and Letters, and the International Music Festival.

Be My AI:The image shows a section of a program with detailed notes about a musical piece by Johann Sebastian Bach, followed by a section about Gabriel Fauré. Here is the transcribed text:

JOHANN SEBASTIAN BACH
Chaconne from Partita No. 2 for Solo Violin in D minor, BWV 1004

A magisterial compendium of compositional styles and instrumental techniques, Bach’s six Sonatas and Partitas for solo violin have delighted listeners ever since they were rediscovered in the mid-1800s. A few earlier composers had written music for unaccompanied violin, but never on the scale of Bach. Nor had anyone achieved such breathtaking contrapuntal and harmonic complexity with a single melodic instrument. As Johann Nikolaus Forkel observed in 1802, Bach “has so combined in a single part all the notes required to make the modulations complete that a second part is neither necessary nor desirable.”

The majestic architecture of the great D-minor Chaconne rests on the sturdiest, and simplest, of foundations. Its 256 bars are supported by a repeated but ever-changing bass line that provides the harmonic underpinning for a sequence of 32 stunningly imaginative variations. The final movement of Bach’s Partita No. 2 is regarded as both a pinnacle of the violin repertoire and a compositional tour de force. Its influence can be traced down the ages to composers as diverse as Brahms, a master of variation and counterpoint; Bartók, whose Solo Violin Sonata builds on the formidable technical demands that Bach imposes on the performer; and, in our time, Lera Auerbach.

GABRIEL FAURÉ
Violin Sonata No. 1 in A major, Op. 13

Gabriel Fauré’s path to immortality was far from easy.

Be My AI:The image shows a section of a program with notes about a musical piece by Camille Saint-Saëns. Here is the transcribed text:

CAMILLE SAINT-SAËNS
Introduction and Rondo Capriccioso in A minor, Op. 28

Today Saint-Saëns’s fame rests on the “Organ” Symphony, the opera Samson et Dalila, the Carnival of the Animals, and a handful of other works. But he was a towering figure in late 19th-century French music. An aesthetic conservative, he felt a special rapport with the violin, stemming from his meeting with the Spanish virtuoso Pablo de Sarasate in the 1850s. It was for Sarasate that he composed two of his concertos for the instrument, as well as the perennially popular Introduction and Rondo Capriccioso.

The slow, soulful Introduction resembles an operatic arioso in its supple rhythms and burnished lyricism. Although grounded in A minor, it establishes the underlying key of the piece, with the violin soaring in flighty, rhapsodic arabesques, mainly heard against a simple accompaniment. The main theme of the Rondo Capriccioso is a lively dance with syncopations that give the music a jaunty and decidedly Spanish flavor: one can almost hear castanets in the background. With the stomping dance rhythms of the second theme, the violin trills, acrobatic leaps, and fast, intricate passagework, Saint-Saëns employs the time-tested techniques with which Saint-Saëns dazzles the listener, and the work ends in a blaze of A-major glory.

© Harry Haskell, 2025
